source: Commonwealth War Graves Commission / UK National Archives
note: Includes UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, India, South Africa, and other Dominion forces. Killed includes died of wounds and disease.

source: Service historique de la Défense / INSEE
note: France lost proportionally more military-age men than any other major power. Figures include those who died of disease and wounds.
German figures are contested between official and independent estimates. Includes military dead from all theaters including Eastern Front.

source: Russian Imperial Archives / Michael Clodfelter, Warfare and Armed Conflicts
note: Russian casualty figures are among the most contested of WWI due to incomplete record-keeping, Revolution disruption, and differing methodologies.
Multi-national army of Austro-Hungary suffered disproportionately on the Eastern Front and at Caporetto. Empire dissolved before final accounting.
Ottoman records are incomplete; disease (especially typhus and cholera) caused a large proportion of deaths. Civilians killed in Armenian Genocide not included here.

source: US Department of Defense / American Battle Monuments Commission
note: US joined April 1917, with large numbers not arriving until mid-1918. Over half US deaths (63,114) were from disease, especially the 1918 influenza pandemic.

source: Commissariato Generale Onoranze Caduti in Guerra / Mark Thompson
note: Italy suffered severely in eleven Isonzo battles and the Caporetto disaster. 275,000 prisoners captured at Caporetto alone.
Serbia had the highest per-capita military losses of any belligerent. The 1915 winter retreat through Albania killed thousands of soldiers and civilians.
Belgium held the Yser front throughout the war with a reduced but effective army after the fall of Antwerp. Belgium also suffered massive civilian occupation hardships.
Total figures vary significantly based on inclusion criteria: disease, prisoners who died, and wounded-who-later-died are counted differently across sources.

source: C. Paul Vincent, The Politics of Hunger (1985) / German estimates
note: British naval blockade caused malnutrition and disease-related civilian deaths in Germany 1914–1919. German wartime estimates (763,000) are generally considered too high; modern estimates range from 400,000–600,000.

source: International Association of Genocide Scholars / Taner Akçam
note: The most contested death toll of WWI. Turkey disputes the 'genocide' characterization and the higher casualty estimates. Most historians accept a minimum of 600,000 deaths; many cite 1–1.2 million.

source: CDC / John Barry, The Great Influenza (2004)
note: While not a direct combat casualty, the 1918 'Spanish Flu' pandemic—spread and amplified by wartime mobilization and troop movements—killed more people than all WWI combat. US alone lost 675,000 civilians.

source: SIPRI / L.F. Haber, The Poisonous Cloud (1986)
note: Gas caused a relatively small proportion of deaths but accounted for a significant proportion of injuries, including many long-term disability cases from mustard gas burns and lung damage.

source: Niall Ferguson, The Pity of War (1998) / Joanna Bourke
note: Approximately 8 million soldiers were held as POWs. Death rates in captivity varied enormously: Russian POWs in Germany fared best; Ottoman POWs died at extremely high rates from disease and neglect.
